context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 6

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 1st April, 2024
Days Covered: 11 and 12 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Failed Induction [10 points]

(a) [5 pts] In Recitation 11 Problem 3, it may seem like we've proved a stronger theorem:
    False Claim. Every graph with n nodes and at most 3n − 6 edges can be 6-colored.
Show that this claim is actually false, by finding some integer n and some graph with n nodes and at most 3n − 6 edges that cannot be 6-colored. Be sure to explain why your example cannot be 6-colored.
(b) [5 pts] Why doesn't the induction argument from Recitation 11 Problem 3(c) work for this stronger claim? Where does the proof break down?


Problem 2. High Chromatic Number Without Triangles [15 points]

A basic example of a graph with chromatic number n is the complete graph on n vertices, that is χ(Kn) = n. This implies that any graph with Kn as a subgraph must have chromatic number at least n. It's a common misconception to think that, conversely, graphs with high chromatic number must contain a large complete subgraph. In this problem we exhibit a counterexample: a graph with chromatic number four that contains no triangle (length-three cycle) and hence no subgraph isomorphic to Kn for n ≥ 3. Namely, let G be the 11-vertex graph below. The reader can verify that G is triangle-free.

[Figure: the Grötzsch graph G, an 11-vertex triangle-free graph with χ(G) = 4. It has a central vertex w, an inner 5-cycle v0, v1, v2, v3, v4, and an outer set of 5 vertices u0, u1, u2, u3, u4, with edges connecting w to each vi and each ui to two non-adjacent vj's.]

(a) [6 pts] Prove that χ(G) ≤ 4.
(b) [3 pts] Assume that we had a coloring of G using only 3 colors. Prove that the coloring must match the partial coloring shown below, possibly after rotating the graph and/or relabeling the three colors.
[Figure: partial 3-coloring of the inner wheel — Blue and Pink on the top two outer vertices, Pink and Blue on the lower two, and Green on the central/bottom vertex, with v0..v4 and w labeled.]
(c) [6 pts] Prove that χ(G) > 3.
    Hint: Part (b) might be helpful.


Problem 3. Find A Pet [13 points]

Hasbro's market research has shown that giving their TV show protagonists animal sidekicks can increase toy sales. Meanwhile, 4 ponies were traveling across Equestria on their latest friendship quest and discovered an old animal sanctuary. Four of the local critters decided to join the group.

The following tables show everypony's and everycritter's preferences (most-favored to least-favored):

| Pony                | Critters (preferences) |
|---------------------|------------------------|
| Hitch Trailblazer   | D, C, B, A             |
| Izzy Moonbow        | A, C, B, D             |
| Sunny Starscout     | C, B, D, A             |
| Zipp Storm          | D, B, C, A             |

| Critter                  | Ponies (preferences) |
|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Arlo the Armadillo       | H, S, Z, I           |
| Beatrice the Beaver      | H, I, S, Z           |
| Cassandra the Capybara   | H, I, Z, S           |
| Draco the Dragonfly      | H, Z, S, I           |

(a) [5 pts] Run the Matching Algorithm with critters proposing to ponies. Please specify the proposals (new and recurring) and reservations/rejections that occur on each day. (We recommend drawing a box around the reserved partner on each day.) You may or may not need all the space given.
(b) [5 pts] Run the Matching Algorithm, this time with ponies proposing to pets.
(c) [3 pts] Prove that any matching pairing H with C is unstable.


Problem 4. Stable Merriment [12 points]

In a stable matching between n Pokémon and n Trainers produced by the Stable Matching Algorithm (with Pokémon applying and Trainers evaluating), call a Pokémon or Trainer merry if they are matched with one of their top ⌈n/2⌉ choices. In this problem, we'll prove that at least one Pokémon or Trainer must be merry.
Let S be the total number of times a Trainer rejects a Pokémon.

(a) [6 pts] Prove that if there are no merry Trainers, then S < n^2 / 2.
(b) [6 pts] Conclude that at least one Pokémon or Trainer must be merry.
    Hint: What can be said about S if there are no merry Pokémon?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, i’m stuck on Problem 2 rn, esp parts (b) and (c). i kinda get that the graph is triangle-free, but idk how to force the 3-coloring pattern.
exchanges.tutor: Welcome! Let's start with what you know. In a valid 3-coloring, what constraint must hold between any two adjacent vertices?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is starting on Problem 2(b) and (c). I should begin by assessing what they already understand before providing any scaffolding. I'll ask them to describe what they know about the structure of the graph and what constraints a 3-coloring must satisfy, using the Socratic method to guide them toward the key insight themselves.

exchanges.student: adjacent vertices can’t have the same color, that part i know. i’m just not seeing how that alone forces the specific pattern in 2(b).
exchanges.tutor: Good. Let's zoom in on the inner 5-cycle (v0 through v4). With only 3 colors available, how many colors must appear on that cycle, and why can't you get away with just 2?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student understands the basic coloring constraint but isn't seeing how it forces the pattern. I should guide them to focus on a specific, concrete part of the graph structure first — the inner 5-cycle. A 5-cycle with 3 colors has very limited valid colorings, and that's where the forcing begins. I'll ask them to think about the inner 5-cycle specifically without giving away the argument.

exchanges.student: i think all 3 have to appear, since a 5-cycle is odd so it’s not 2-colorable. so 2 colors fail somewhere bc alternating around the cycle breaks at the end.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. Now, 5 vertices and 3 colors — by pigeonhole, at least one color appears more than once. What does the no-adjacent-same-color rule tell you about where those repeated-color vertices must sit on the cycle?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified that the 5-cycle requires all 3 colors. Now I should push them to think about what this means more concretely — specifically, since 5 vertices use 3 colors, by pigeonhole one color must appear at least twice. And since the cycle is odd, the coloring pattern is quite constrained. I'll nudge them toward thinking about how the colors must be distributed around the cycle.

exchanges.student: they can’t be next to each other, so the repeated color has to be on nonadjacent vertices. on a 5-cycle that means distance 2 or 3 apart, which is basically the same idea around the cycle.
exchanges.tutor: Good. So what must the exact color distribution be across the 5 vertices — how many times does each color appear? Think about whether 2-2-1 or some other split is possible.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track. Now I want them to realize that on a 5-cycle, any valid 3-coloring has a very specific structure: one color appears exactly twice and the other two appear once each, OR the distribution could be 2-2-1. I should push them to think about the exact distribution and then how that constrains which vertices get which colors — leading toward the observation that the pattern is essentially unique up to rotation and color relabeling.

exchanges.student: i think it has to be 2-2-1, bc 5 total and 3 colors used. 3-1-1 seems impossible on a 5-cycle since if one color showed up 3 times, two of those would end up adjacent i think?
exchanges.tutor: Right. So you have two colors each appearing twice, and one appearing once. On the 5-cycle, where can the lone vertex (the color appearing once) be relative to the two pairs?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the 2-2-1 distribution and gave a reasonable argument for ruling out 3-1-1. This is good progress. Now I should push them to think about what the 2-2-1 pattern means for the structure — specifically, the two pairs of same-colored vertices must be non-adjacent, and on a 5-cycle this heavily constrains the arrangement. This is the key step toward showing the pattern is unique up to rotation/relabeling.

exchanges.student: i’m guessing it has to be between the pairs when u go around the cycle, like A B A C B up to rotation? but i’m not fully sure why there isn’t another arrangement.
exchanges.tutor: Good intuition. Try to write out a different arrangement of 2-2-1 on the 5-cycle and check if it satisfies the no-adjacent-same-color rule. What do you find?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has intuited the correct pattern but isn't sure why other arrangements are impossible. Rather than explaining it, I should ask them to try to construct an alternative arrangement and see if it leads to a contradiction. This guided discovery will solidify their understanding.

exchanges.student: if i try A A B C B, that already fails bc the first two are adjacent. and A B B A C also fails. so yeah, it seems the only valid pattern up to rotation is A B A C B.
exchanges.tutor: Nice. Now bring in vertex w, which is connected to all five vertices of the inner cycle. Given the coloring A B A C B on the cycle, what color can w be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now convinced themselves that the 5-cycle coloring is unique up to rotation and relabeling. This is great progress. Now I should connect this to the full graph — specifically, how does w (the center vertex connected to all vi's) interact with this coloring? That's the next step toward forcing the full pattern in part (b).

exchanges.student: w touches A, B, and C on the cycle, so it can’t be any of those. wait, that sounds like a 4th color is forced already? i’m confused bc part (b) says assume a 3-coloring.
exchanges.tutor: You're actually stumbling onto part (c) right there — hold that thought! For part (b), step back: if w must get a 4th color, what does that tell you about whether a 3-coloring of the whole graph is possible? And how does that connect to what part (b) is actually asking you to show?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a critical and insightful observation — they're essentially stumbling onto the proof of part (c) while working on part (b). I should clarify the logical structure: part (b) asks to show that IF a 3-coloring exists, it must look a certain way. The student's observation actually points toward part (c). I need to redirect them: in part (b), w is not connected to all vi's — let me double-check the graph structure. Actually, in the Grötzsch graph, w IS connected to all five outer ui vertices, not to the inner vi's. The inner 5-cycle is v0-v4, w connects to the ui's. Let me re-read: 'w to each vi' — actually the problem says w connects to each vi (inner cycle). So the student's observation is correct and IS the contradiction for part (c). For part (b), they need to reason about the outer vertices ui and how they connect to the vi's. I need to clarify the graph structure with the student and redirect them toward part (b)'s actual argument about the outer vertices.

exchanges.student: ohh wait, then a 3-coloring of the whole graph isn’t possible at all. so now i’m kinda confused why part (b) says “assume we had a coloring using only 3 colors” and prove it matches that partial coloring.
exchanges.tutor: Good catch — parts (b) and (c) work together logically. Part (b) works by contradiction's setup: *if* a 3-coloring existed, it would be forced into a specific pattern. Part (c) then shows even that forced pattern leads to a contradiction. So look at the partial coloring figure again — which vertices does it actually show, and which are left uncolored?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is confused about the logical structure of parts (b) and (c). They're right that if w connects to all vi's and the cycle uses all 3 colors, then w can't be colored — but I need to be careful here. Let me re-examine the Grötzsch graph structure from the problem description: 'w to each vi and each ui to two non-adjacent vj's.' So w connects to each vi (inner 5-cycle vertices). This means the student's reasoning is correct — w sees all 3 colors on the cycle. But part (b) says to prove the coloring must match a partial coloring that DOES include w with a color. So maybe I'm misreading the graph, or the partial coloring shown in part (b) only shows the outer ui vertices and w, not the inner vi's with w. Looking again at the figure description: 'partial 3-coloring of the inner wheel — Blue and Pink on the top two outer vertices, Pink and Blue on the lower two, and Green on the central/bottom vertex' — the 'central/bottom vertex' with Green might be w, and the outer vertices shown are some of the ui's, not all vi's. Actually, the Grötzsch graph has w connected to all ui's (outer), and the vi's form the inner 5-cycle connected to w... wait, different sources describe it differently. The problem says 'w to each vi' so w connects to all 5 inner cycle vertices. The student's contradiction argument then applies directly, which IS part (c). Part (b) must be about a partial coloring that doesn't include all vertices. I should clarify to the student that parts (b) and (c) work together: (b) shows IF 3-coloring exists it must look a certain way, and (c) shows even that forced pattern leads to contradiction via the outer vertices. The key is that part (b) may not be considering w's connections in the same way, or the partial coloring shown only covers some vertices. I should ask the student to look more carefully at which vertices are shown in the partial coloring figure for part (b) and what the hint structure suggests about the logical flow.

exchanges.student: ok yeah that makes more sense. i think the figure is only forcing some of the vertices, not the whole graph — like the inner cycle and maybe some outer ones? i’m not 100% sure which labels are in the partial coloring.
exchanges.tutor: Based on what you've worked out, you already know the inner 5-cycle must follow the A B A C B pattern. So for part (b), the next step is: given that forced cycle coloring, can you figure out what color each outer vertex ui must be, given how each ui connects to the inner cycle?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is uncertain about the partial coloring figure. I should help them focus on what we've already established and build from there. We know the inner 5-cycle must be colored A B A C B (up to rotation/relabeling), and w connects to all vi's so w must avoid all 3 colors — that's the contradiction for part (c). But for part (b), the partial coloring shown involves the inner 5-cycle vi's and w. Since the problem says the partial coloring shows w with Green and some outer ui vertices colored, the logical flow must be: first fix the inner cycle coloring (which we've done), then show the outer ui vertices' colors are also forced. I should guide the student to think about what's forced step by step, starting from what we know.
